text: They say that a camel is nothing but a
horse designed by commits. And funnily
enough, that was the first thing that
popped into my mind after watching the
Minecraft movie. It's what happens when
a bunch of people with seemingly no
creative impulses in their entire bodies
are asked to come up with a generic
comedy adventure flick to take advantage
of the growing video game adaptation
trend and milk a popular IP that they're
only vaguely familiar with, while also
trying to tick as many corporatemandated
boxes as humanly possible. What you end
up with is an absolutely forgettable
movie that does nothing but function as
a 2-hour commercial for the game.
Starring a bunch of bored looking actors
who probably spent 90% of the shoot
standing in a big green room with no
idea what they were supposed to be
reacting to. Loaded down with jokes that
aren't funny, too many characters that
have nothing to do, and the most paper
thin excuse for a plot that I've seen
since Borderlands. Minecraft is where
film making and imagination goes to die.
the logical endpoint of lazy IP
exploiting Hollywood executives who are
driven entirely by numbers and
statistics. A film that was made purely
out of necessity with nothing even
resembling passion or interest from
anyone involved. Let's get this one over
with quick because Minecraft deserves
nothing better. The movie stars Jack
Black as Steve, a guy who quits his
deadend job to mine for treasure and
adventure.
No, wait. Actually, it stars Jason Mimoa
as Garrett, a washedup 80s video game
protetéé. No, wait, actually, it stars
Sebastian Hansen as some kid who moves
to a new town with his sister and finds
a portal into another world. Are you
serious? Yeah, in case you hadn't
noticed, the script's a bit confused
about who the main character is even
supposed to be. Conventional wisdom
would suggest it should be Steve since
he's the protagonist of the games and
everything, but I guess at some point
the studio realized that no one under
the age of 30 knows or cares who Jack
Black is. So they brought in Jason
Mimoa, who's younger and cooler, to play
a big beefy jock type character. And I
guess maybe the story was going to have
the two of them joining forces to take
on the bad guys or something. But then
some corporate suit decided that the
movie would test better with children if
they had a kid as the main character.
And so Henry was written into the story
with no particular arc or personality in
minds. And then the chief diversity
officer decided they needed more
representation. So they threw in a
random black lady who has absolutely no
reason to be there and contributes
nothing to the
story. [ __ ] Great work, guys. Be sure
to give yourselves an extra large bonus
this year. Anyway, the plot, such as it
is, unfolds thusly. Steve finds a
magical [ __ ] crystal that opens a
portal to the world of Minecraft. But
then accidentally discovers an evil
underworld inhabited by an army of evil
pigs who imprison him, but not before he
gives the crystal to his dog, who
escapes back through the portal into the
real world and hides it. The crystal
eventually falls into the hands of
Garrett, who tries to sell it in his
video game store before Henry takes it
and uses it to cross over into the world
of Minecraft, too. Oh yeah, and somehow
Garrett, Henry's sister, and some random
black lady also get pulled in with them.
But oh no, the crystal gets damaged. So
they have to find a replacement. But the
evil pig lady wants it for herself so
she can take over the world or
something. So she releases Steve on the
promise that he'll get it and bring it
back to her. So naturally, Steve
immediately betrays her and joins the
gang because why the [ __ ] wouldn't he?
And together they work to defeat the
evil pig army, open the portal back to
the real world, and go home. And I know
it probably seems like I'm speedrunning
through the plot on this one, but
honestly, I'm not because basically [ __ ]
all happens in this movie. It's the same
basic [ __ ] template you've seen in a
dozen other films just like it, and it
does absolutely nothing new or
unexpected at any point. There's a kind
of half-hearted attempt to emulate the
moral messaging of The Lego Movie, the
conflict between the chaotic, messy
creativity, and the crushing conformity
of order and control, but it's so
loosely explored and easily forgotten
that it never really makes an
impression. The evil pig Queen Lady
whose name I can't even be bothered to
look up, is by far the weakest villain
I've ever seen in a film like this.
voiced by an absolutely dreadful actress
who sounds like she's reading her lines
off a teleprompter with no idea of the
context or meaning behind them. Jack
Black plays a loud, shouty, obnoxious
[ __ ] who's trying so hard to be funny
you can practically see him sweating
with the effort. So basically every Jack
Black movie these days. Then the rest of
the cast are mostly forgettable. Henry
makes no impression as the protagonist.
His sister isn't developed enough to
care about. And although random black
lady isn't nearly as annoying as you
might think from the trailer, she really
doesn't have much reason to be there
either. The script has no idea what to
do with her. She's just kind of there in
the background most of the time. Jason
Momoa is the only positive of the film
if you can call it that. What few laughs
there are come mostly from him. And
although he doesn't look like he wants
to be there much, he's at least got
enough charisma and comic timing to make
you crack a smile once in a while.
Honestly, if you've got really young
kids that are big fans of the game and
not inclined to think too much about
what's actually happening, there's
probably just enough here to keep them
happy. But anyone with a mental age in
double digits is absolutely going to
struggle with this one. And the funny
thing is, none of this really matters.
Minecraft is such a massive IP that this
film's probably going to rake in a
billion dollars, and nobody is going to
remember it 5 minutes after it leaves
theaters.
Anyway, that's all I've got for
today. Go away now.
